original.rus: Товарищи, есть установка весело встретить Новый год

general.text:
This is a key Ogurtsov line and an important tone-setter for the film. The humor comes from the clash between festive content (“to celebrate the New Year merrily”) and rigid, quasi-bureaucratic phrasing (“Comrades,” “there is an instruction / directive”). It sounds as if joy is not spontaneous but administratively mandated from above.

What should be preserved:
- the Soviet-official, managerial register;
- the internal contradiction between bureaucratic form and festive content;
- the dry seriousness with which “having fun” is formulated.

Translation risk:
If this is rendered as just “let’s celebrate the New Year merrily,” half the joke is lost. The point is that this is not a friendly invitation but a top-down directive-like formulation.

reference.eng: Comrades, we have our instructions to celebrate the New Year merrily.

zero.1:
Comrades, there is a directive to greet
the New Year cheerfully.
zero.2:
Comrades, we have a directive to
welcome the New Year cheerfully.
zero.3:
Comrades, we have a directive to
celebrate the New Year cheerfully.
zero.4:
Comrades, there is a directive
to welcome the New Year cheerfully.

original.rus: Бабу-Ягу со стороны брать не будем — воспитаем в своём коллективе

general.text:
This is one of the film’s best-known lines. What makes it funny is not just the fairy-tale reference, but the social type behind it. Baba Yaga is not only a folkloric witch here; she also evokes the idea of a nasty, malicious, deeply unpleasant woman of the kind Soviet audiences would easily recognize as already present in many workplaces and “collectives.” The joke is that Ogurtsov speaks as if this were a normal staffing or educational matter: they will not “bring in” a Baba Yaga from outside, they will “cultivate” one within their own collective.

So the line works on two levels at once:
- Baba Yaga as a classic fairy-tale villainess;
- Baba Yaga as a recognizable human type: a toxic, troublemaking woman whom any audience might imagine as already available in-house.

What should be avoided:
- making the line too fantastical or fairy-tale-like, because the humor depends on its workplace/social logic.

reference.eng: We won’t bring in a witch from outside — we’ll train one in-house.

zero.1: - We won’t hire a Baba Yaga from outside, we’ll raise one in our own collective!
zero.2:
- We won’t be hiring Baba Yaga
from outside—
we’ll raise one within our collective!
zero.3:
- We won’t hire Baba Yaga
from outside—
we’ll raise one in our own collective!
zero.4:
- We won't
take a Baba Yaga from outside,
we'll raise one within our own collective!

original.rus: коротенько так, минут на сорок, больше, я думаю, не надо…

general.text:
This is a classic Ogurtsov line built on bureaucratic absurdity and comic understatement. The key joke is the word «коротенько» (“briefly,” but in a diminutive, almost cozy form), immediately followed by “forty minutes,” which is obviously the opposite of brief in the context of a festive New Year show. The humor comes from the mismatch between the speaker’s smug sense of moderation and the audience’s clear understanding that this is intolerably long and hopelessly inappropriate.

The line is also funny because it condenses Ogurtsov’s whole worldview: he genuinely believes that inserting an official lecture into a celebration is reasonable, tasteful, and even restrained. His tone is not aggressive but self-satisfied, managerial, and completely tone-deaf to the occasion.

Translation risk:
If «коротенько» is translated too flatly, the contrast weakens. The line needs some sense of faux-modesty or patronizing “just a little short talk,” so that “forty minutes” lands as the punchline. The pacing matters too: the briefness claim should come before the duration, because that is where the comic reversal happens.

reference.eng: about forty minutes, I don’t think we need any more than that…

zero.1:
And briefly—about
forty minutes; more, I think,
isn’t necessary—
zero.2:
And then briefly—about
forty minutes, no more,
I think that’s enough—
zero.3:
And then, briefly—about
forty minutes, no more, I think
that won’t be necessary—
zero.4:
And briefly—about forty minutes,
no more, I think,

original.rus: — Лектор готов? — Готов лектор, давно готов

general.text:
The joke here is that «готов» can mean not only “ready” but also, colloquially, “done for / smashed / drunk.” The exchange is funny because Ogurtsov is asking in a dry administrative sense whether the lecturer is ready to appear, while the answer can be heard as cheerfully confirming that the lecturer is already completely drunk.

So the line works through ambiguity:
- surface meaning: “Is the lecturer ready?” — “Yes, the lecturer is ready, has been ready for a while”;
- comic subtext: “ready” = already drunk / in no condition.

That double reading is what makes the line memorable. The second phrase, «давно готов», heightens the joke: not merely drunk, but apparently drunk for some time already. It also keeps the brisk, production-style rhythm of backstage coordination, which contrasts nicely with the lecturer’s likely actual state.

Translation risk:
If translated only as “ready,” the alcoholic joke may disappear. If translated only as “drunk,” the administrative setup disappears. Ideally the target version should preserve, or recreate, that double sense: official readiness on the surface, intoxication underneath.

reference.eng: — Is the lecturer ready? — Oh, he’s ready. He’s been ready for quite a while.

zero.1:
Is the lecturer ready?
- The lecturer is ready—been ready a long time!
zero.2:
Is the lecturer ready?
- The lecturer is ready—ready a long time
zero.3:
Is the lecturer ready?
- The lecturer is ready—been ready for a long time!
zero.4:
Is the lecturer ready?
- The lecturer's ready—ready long ago!

original.rus: Но стоит только нам взять телескоп! И посмотреть вооружённым глазом!..

general.text:
This line is funny because it is mock-scientific pomp collapsing into nonsense. The lecturer is trying to sound elevated, learned, and impressive, but instead produces a pseudo-academic phrase that is absurd on inspection. The key comic phrase is «вооружённым глазом», a mangled variation on the standard expression «невооружённым глазом» (“with the naked eye”). Once a telescope has been introduced, the phrase becomes a tipsy pseudo-logical invention: the “eye” is now “armed.”

That makes the line funny in at least three ways:
- drunken distortion of learned speech;
- parody of popular-science rhetoric;
- lexical deformation of an idiom the audience would recognize immediately.

The humor depends on the audience hearing that this is almost a proper phrase, but not quite. He sounds like someone reaching for scientific authority and producing damaged language instead.

Translation risk:
If rendered too plainly, the malformed idiom disappears. The line needs to preserve the sense of a familiar expression being incorrectly “upgraded” into mock-scientific language. The lecturer should sound as if he believes he is becoming more precise and scholarly, while actually becoming more ridiculous.

eng.nb: Things_to_avoid: A literal calque such as “armed eye” does not quite work in English; it sounds stranger and more surreal than the Russian. NB: The chosen solution (“aided eye”) works by echoing the English phrase “the unaided eye.” It preserves the slightly wrong, mock-scientific feel without overdoing the oddness.

reference.eng: But all we have to do is take a telescope! And look with the aided eye!..

zero.1:
But we only need to take a telescope...
And look with the armed eye,
zero.2:
But it’s enough for us to take
a telescope...
and look with the armed
eye,
zero.3:
But one only has to take
a telescope...
And look with the armed eye,
zero.4:
But as soon as we take
a telescope...
And look with an armed
eye,

original.rus: Как мы уже видим две звёздочки, три звёздочки, четыре звёздочки… Лучше всего, конечно, пять звёздочек!

eng.text: In the USSR, the best cognac was known as five-star cognac. The English line keeps the original star-based progression, but this cultural association may need a translator’s note because it will not be obvious to most viewers.

reference.eng: As we can already see, two stars, three stars, four stars… But of course five stars is best! [The best cognac in USSR was known as 5*]

zero.1:
and we already see two little stars,
three little stars...
Four little stars; best of all,
of course, five little stars!
zero.2:
and we already see two
little stars, three little stars...
Four little stars—and best of all,
of course, five little stars!

and we already see two
little stars, three little stars...

of course, five little stars!

stars, three stars...

original.rus: Есть ли жизнь на Марсе, нет ли жизни на Марсе — это науке неизвестно. Наука ещё пока не в курсе дела

reference.eng: Whether there is life on Mars or not, that is unknown to science. Science still isn’t up to speed on the matter.

zero.1:
Is there life on Mars, is there no
life on Mars—science does not know.
Science is not yet in the know.
